chapter_summary: Chapter 2 argues that a shared body of knowledge is not merely a pedagogical preference but a foundational civil right and a prerequisite for democracy. The author contends that intellectual capital, distributed through common schooling, is the only mechanism that can ensure both economic independence and the preservation of liberty against political corruption.

claims.text: A shared body of knowledge is a fundamental requirement for a functioning democracy.
claims.source_passage: The need in a democracy to teach children a shared body of knowledge was explained many years ago by Thomas Jefferson when he described his bill “to diffuse knowledge more generally through the mass of the people.”

claims.text: A common grade-school education serves as a safeguard against aristocracy by giving every talent an equal chance to excel.
claims.source_passage: It would be a place where every talent would be given an equal chance to excel, where “every fibre would be eradicated of ancient and future aristocracy; and a foundation laid for a government truly republican.”

claims.text: History education is the most important component of primary schooling for qualifying citizens to judge the actions of their rulers.
claims.source_passage: Of the views of this [education] law, none is more important, none more legitimate, than of rendering the people the safe, as they are the ultimate, guardians of their own liberty. For this purpose, the reading in the first stage [of schooling]... is proposed to he chiefly historical.

claims.text: Every government contains seeds of corruption and degeneracy, and only an educated populace can serve as a safe depository for the government's power.
claims.source_passage: Every government degenerates when trusted to the rulers of the people alone. The people themselves therefore are its only safe depositories. And to render even them safe, their minds must he improved to a certain degree.

claims.text: Democracy requires a 'common school' to provide children with the knowledge and skills necessary for economic independence.
claims.source_passage: Jefferson’s conception was later seconded by Horace Mann, who argued that democracy required a “common school” to provide all children equally with the knowledge and skills that would keep them economically independent and free.

claims.text: Community existence is predicated on members possessing things in common, specifically aims, beliefs, aspirations, and knowledge.
claims.source_passage: Men live in a community in virtue of the things which they have in common; and communication is the way in which they come to possess things in common. What they must have in common are aims, beliefs, aspirations, knowledge—a common understanding.

claims.text: Shared frames of reference are necessary common property to prevent social inequality resulting from differences in competence.
claims.source_passage: It is a central tenet of popular enlightenment that shared frames of reference must be the common property of all the people—indeed must be an integral part of general education—to escape avoidable differences in competence that can result in social inequality and be abused by undemocratic forces.

claims.text: Individuals who lack the background information assumed in public discourse are effectively excluded from full participation in a democratic community.
claims.source_passage: Those who do not share the background information taken for granted in public discourse will often overlook the point or miss the meaning. Newcomers to a country who are not immersed in its frames of reference often remain outsiders because others cannot take for granted what they know and can do.

claims.text: Common background knowledge serves as the core network for national communication, enabling the interpretation of complex messages and new ideas.
claims.source_passage: Common background knowledge is thus at the core of a national network of communication between members of a democratic community. It makes it possible to fathom complex messages, and to interpret new ideas, situations and challenges.

claims.text: In the late industrial era, shared knowledge has become an economic imperative, not just a political one.
claims.source_passage: In the late industrial era, the need for common learnings has acquired a more than political urgency. The purely economic need for a high level of shared knowledge among workers has led to an unforeseen strengthening of democratic political principles in previously nondemocratic regimes.

claims.text: The 1917 Russian Revolution was malleable because it was carried out by uneducated peasants, whereas modern democratic revolutions are more durable due to universal literacy.
claims.source_passage: the Soviet state had been forced by economic imperatives to make its people literate, whereas the revolution of 1917 had been carried out by uneducated and therefore malleable peasants.

claims.text: The modern economic world requires stronger schooling for citizens to sustain themselves in the workplace than in any previous era.
claims.source_passage: Economically, citizens now require an especially strong schooling to sustain themselves in the workplace. To express the socioeconomic implications of education in the modern world, sociologists have devised the useful concept of “intellectual capital.”

claims.text: The Soviet state was forced by economic imperatives to make its people literate.
claims.source_passage: the Soviet state had been forced by economic imperatives to make its people literate

claims.text: Citizens must be educated in order to be protected from their rulers and from themselves.
claims.source_passage: Politically, citizens must still be educated in order to be protected from their rulers, and, as Jefferson indicated, from themselves.

claims.text: Intellectual capital is the key to American competitiveness and prosperity.
claims.source_passage: “Intellectual capital” has been described by Secretary of Labor Robert Reich as the key to American competitiveness and prosperity.

claims.text: Intellectual capital determines social class, success or failure in school, and even psychological and physical health.
claims.source_passage: Sociologists have shown that intellectual capital (i.e., knowledge) operates in almost every sphere of modern society to determine social class, success or failure in school, and even psychological and physical health.

claims.text: It takes knowledge to make knowledge, just as it takes money to make money.
claims.source_passage: Just as it takes money to make money, it takes knowledge to make knowledge.

claims.text: The Biblical paradox of the Matthew Effect (Matthew 13:12) holds more inexorably for intellectual capital than for money capital.
claims.source_passage: The paradox holds more inexorably for intellectual than for money capital.

claims.text: Children who lack relevant experience and vocabulary in early school years fall further and further behind.
claims.source_passage: But those children who arrive at school lacking the relevant experience and vocabulary—they see not, neither do they understand. They fall further and further behind.

claims.text: Lack of early stimulation depresses children's IQs.
claims.source_passage: Lack of stimulation has depressed their IQs.

claims.text: Early inequity in the distribution of intellectual capital is the single most important source of avoidable injustice in a free society.
claims.source_passage: In short, an early inequity in the distribution of intellectual capital may be the single most important source of avoidable injustice in a free society.

claims.text: Initial knowledge deficits in children can be remedied in the early years of preschool and primary school.
claims.source_passage: Despite claims to the contrary, we know that the initial knowledge deficits of children can be remedied in the early years of preschool and primary school

claims.text: Only knowledge that constitutes the shared intellectual currency of a society enables effective communication and learning.
claims.source_passage: not just any knowledge will work—only that knowledge which constitutes the shared intellectual currency of the society.

claims.text: The American educational theory that all-purpose intellectual competencies are independent of specific content is contradicted by research.
claims.source_passage: But when this tool metaphor has been taken apart and examined for its literal content, its highly exaggerated claims have been powerfully contradicted by research

claims.text: The 'all-purpose tools' (skill-centric) model of education has shown itself to be ineffective after sixty years of use.
claims.source_passage: and after six decades, it has shown itself to be ineffective.

claims.text: The educational 'tool metaphor' and its indifference to specific knowledge have nullified the progress of school integration and the civil rights movement.
claims.source_passage: It is probably not an exaggeration to say that the broad sway of this theoretical mistake has all but nullified the bright promise of school integration and the civil rights movement.

claims.text: Under a process-dominated educational theory, the home environment is the decisive influence on a child's academic outcomes.
claims.source_passage: Coleman’s finding is highly consistent with the idea of the importance of intellectual capital, since under the current process-dominated theory it is the home, by default, and not the school that supplies most of the intellectual capital which enables children to acquire more.

claims.text: When the home is the dominant influence in education, schooling fails to advance economically and educationally depressed groups.
claims.source_passage: When the home is the dominant influence in education, it follows that economically and educationally depressed groups will not be greatly advanced by schooling.

claims.text: The relative influence of the home versus the school on student outcomes depends on the specific educational context and philosophy in place.
claims.source_passage: Bagley’s Determinism in Education analyzed results of the Army Alpha tests given in World War I... Bagley was interested in correlating the IQ scores of large groups with the states they came from... Bagley’s study powerfully illustrates that researches into the social outcomes of schooling and into the relative influence of the home greatly depend on the educational context in which those researches are conducted.

claims.text: In the early 20th century, traditional schools were the decisive factor in determining average IQ and general competence, outweighing the influence of family income.
claims.source_passage: Thus, during an era when American schools operated on traditional rather than progressivist principles, excellent schools, even more than the home or the family income (family income of African-Americans in 1900 was not high), made a decisive difference in average IQ and general competence.

claims.text: Intellectual capital is the primary tool for adaptation in the modern world.
claims.source_passage: Indeed, as I shall be concerned to show in Chapter 5, intellectual capital is itself the great all-purpose tool of adaptation in the modern world.

claims.text: Shared knowledge among students is essential for a classroom to be effective.
claims.source_passage: Why is shared knowledge among students essential to an effective classroom?

claims.text: The ability to learn new information depends on the ability to accommodate that new information to existing knowledge.
claims.source_passage: Psychological research has shown that the ability to learn something new depends on an ability to accommodate the new thing to the already known.

claims.text: Relevant background knowledge functions as a stock of potential analogies that enable new ideas to be assimilated.
claims.source_passage: Relevant background knowledge can be conceived as a stock of potential analogies that enable new ideas to be assimilated.

claims.text: Experts learn new things faster than novices because their rich background knowledge provides more means for capturing new ideas.
claims.source_passage: Experts in any field learn new things faster than novices do, because their rich, highly accessible background knowledge gives them a greater variety of means for capturing the new ideas.

claims.text: The enabling function of prior knowledge is essential at every stage of learning.
claims.source_passage: This enabling function of relevant prior knowledge is essential at every stage of learning.

claims.text: A classroom group cannot move forward together until all students possess the taken-for-granted knowledge required for the next step.
claims.source_passage: A classroom of twenty-five to thirty children cannot move forward as a group until all students have gained the taken-for-granted knowledge necessary for “getting” the next step in learning.

claims.text: Pausing a class to provide missing background knowledge to lagging students slows the progress for better-prepared students.
claims.source_passage: If the class must pause too often while its lagging members are given background knowledge they should have gained in earlier grades, the progress of the class is bound to be excruciatingly slow for better-prepared students.

claims.text: Teachers who press ahead without addressing the knowledge gaps of lagging students cause those students to fall further behind.
claims.source_passage: If, on the other hand, instead of slowing down the class for laggards, the teacher presses ahead, the less-prepared students are bound to be left further and further behind.

claims.text: Placing students in different 'ability' tracks does not solve the structural problem of missing background knowledge.
claims.source_passage: In the American context, this familiar problem is not adequately overcome by placing students in different “ability” tracks, because the basic structural problem has little to do with ability.

claims.text: Children cannot keep up in first grade without the specific knowledge and vocabulary assumed by the teacher and peers.
claims.source_passage: Implicit in the ready-to-learn principle is the (true) assumption that children cannot keep up in first grade unless they arrive there with the knowledge and vocabulary that will enable them to participate actively in the class.

claims.text: The 'ready-to-learn' principle should be extended to every grade level, not just first grade.
claims.source_passage: logical consistency would require that we extend the readiness-to-learn principle to second graders and third graders, and so on.

claims.text: Democratic education requires that every student be guaranteed the academic preparation needed for their specific grade level.
claims.source_passage: For it is a fundamental requirement of democratic education that every student who enters a class at the beginning of the year should be vouchsafed the academic preparation needed to gain the knowledge and skills to be taught in that year.

claims.text: Schools have a duty to provide children with the knowledge and skills for progress regardless of their home environment.
claims.source_passage: it is the duty of schools to provide each child with the knowledge and skills requisite for academic progress—regardless of home background.

claims.text: Classroom learning is most effective when students share enough common reference points to learn steadily.
claims.source_passage: For effective classroom learning to take place, class members need to share enough common reference points to enable all students to learn steadily, albeit at differing rates and in response to varied approaches.

claims.text: A lack of common academic preparation in American classrooms causes slower learning progress compared to other systems.
claims.source_passage: when this requisite commonality of preparation is lacking, as it is in most American classrooms today, the progress of learning will be slow compared with that of educational systems which do achieve commonality of academic preparation within the classroom.

claims.text: The variability in children’s educational background and levels of preparation is a greater problem for teaching than the diversity in their social and cultural backgrounds.
claims.source_passage: The error … is the assumption that it is the diversity in children’s social and cultural backgrounds that poses the greatest problem for teaching. In fact, a far greater problem is variability in children’s educational background, and thus in their levels of preparation for learning the academic curriculum.

claims.text: The learning gap in academic performance between American and Asian students grows wider over time, from sixth to eleventh grade.
claims.source_passage: Subsequent work by Stevenson and his colleagues has shown that this gap grows wider over time, putting American students much further behind their Asian peers by eleventh grade than they were in sixth.

claims.text: The achievement gap between advantaged and disadvantaged students inside American schools follows a widening 'funnel shape' similar to the international gap.
claims.source_passage: The funnel shape of this widening international gap has an eerie similarity to the funnel shape of the widening gap inside American schools between advantaged and disadvantaged students as they progress through the grades.

claims.text: The lack of academic commonality in American classrooms slows down the progress of the class as a whole.
claims.source_passage: A plausible explanation for the widening in both cases is that a lack of academic commonality in the American classroom not only slows down the class as a whole, thus making us lag behind other countries...

claims.text: Incoherent schooling forces students to rely on background knowledge gained at home, creating a discrepancy between lucky and unlucky students.
claims.source_passage: also creates an increasing discrepancy between students who are lucky enough to have gained the needed background knowledge at home and those who have to depend mainly on what they get sporadically in school.

claims.text: The learning of advantaged students snowballs upon initial advantage, while the learning of disadvantaged students never gathers momentum in an incoherent school system.
claims.source_passage: The learning of luckier students snowballs upon their initial advantage, while that of the less fortunate ones, dependent on what the incoherent American school offers, never even begins to gather momentum.

claims.text: The lack of shared knowledge among students creates both a national excellence gap and a fairness gap.
claims.source_passage: The lack of shared knowledge among American students not only holds back their average progress, creating a national excellence gap, but, more drastically, holds back disadvantaged students, thus creating a fairness gap as well.

claims.text: Misbehavior at the top of the academic range is driven by boredom, while at the bottom it is driven by boredom compounded with humiliation.
claims.source_passage: the most consistent problems of misbehavior occur among students at the top and at the bottom of the academic range, the one group antagonized by boredom, the other by boredom compounded with humiliation—emotions that are induced and exacerbated by lack of shared knowledge in the classroom.

claims.text: The pathology of truancy is located in the intellectual transmission of the school rather than in the children themselves.
claims.source_passage: He concludes that “the pathology is mostly in the intellectual transmission and not in the children.”

claims.text: Giving children enabling knowledge is inherently motivational and liberates a natural eagerness to learn.
claims.source_passage: More positively, giving young children enabling knowledge is inherently motivational; it liberates their natural eagerness to learn.

claims.text: Human children possess a general instinct to learn that impels them toward membership in the adult community.
claims.source_passage: It is arguable, and indeed it has been argued since Aristotle, that the language instinct is part of a more general instinct to learn, which impels youngsters toward becoming members of the adult community.

claims.text: Boredom and indifference in children are the result of their natural curiosity being systematically thwarted.
claims.source_passage: Their natural curiosity and eagerness are inborn instincts that have to be systematically thwarted in order to make the children bored or indifferent.

claims.text: Introducing a coherent, content-oriented curriculum in an elementary school typically results in a 70 percent increase in library use.
claims.source_passage: In the first year during which a coherent, content-oriented curriculum is introduced into an American elementary school, library use rises typically by 70 percent.

claims.text: Enabling knowledge acts as 'active tentacles' that foster curiosity, not just 'passive hooks' that wait for information.
claims.source_passage: Giving children enabling knowledge gives them not just passive hooks but also active tentacles.

claims.text: The belief that local American school districts or individual schools possess a coherent plan for teaching content is a misleading myth.
claims.source_passage: But despite the democratic virtue of that principle, the idea that there exists a coherent plan for teaching content within the local district, or even within the individual school, is a gravely misleading myth.

claims.text: American elementary schools are characterized by curricular chaos that most people are unaware of.
claims.source_passage: The curricular chaos of the American elementary school is a feature of our public education that few people are even remotely aware of.

claims.text: School principals are generally unable to identify the minimal specific content that all children at a given grade level are expected to learn.
claims.source_passage: he discovered that no principal in his district could tell him what minimal content each child in a grade was expected to learn.

claims.text: State and local 'curriculum guides' fail to define the specific content required at each grade level despite their physical bulk.
claims.source_passage: Many states and local districts have produced thick documents called “curriculum guides,” which, for all their thickness, do not answer the simple question “What specific content are all children at a grade level required to learn?”

claims.text: A list of textbooks does not constitute a defined minimal curriculum because teachers often omit different topics from those books.
claims.source_passage: But a list of textbooks does not provide an answer to the question about minimal content any more than does a thick pile of district guidelines.

claims.text: The lack of curricular specificity makes it impossible for teachers to know what their students have learned in previous grades.
claims.source_passage: Different topics are omitted by different teachers, thereby making it impossible for the children’s later teachers to know what has been covered at earlier grades—they cannot be sure what their students know and do not know.

claims.text: The school year is the essential unit for effective curricular planning.
claims.source_passage: I emphasize in this context the importance of defining content for a particular grade level, since the school year is the critical unit for curricular planning.

claims.text: Vague, multi-year curricular goals prevent effective monitoring and accountability for both students and teachers.
claims.source_passage: Vague, multiyear goals make neither the student nor the teacher responsible for gaps; a gap is always something that should have been filled in some other year! Close monitoring becomes impossible. Accountability cannot be maintained for either students or teachers.

claims.text: The language in district curriculum guides is intentionally vague to avoid infringing upon teacher prerogatives regarding specific content.
claims.source_passage: These words disclose a characteristic reluctance on the part of district guides to impinge upon the teachers’ prerogatives—in this case, by not stating which national symbols, major holidays, historical figures, and historical events the local curriculum makers have in view.

claims.text: Without specific content standards, teachers following the same general guidelines will instruct students in completely different information.
claims.source_passage: But, in the absence of specifics, is there any reason to believe that different teachers will respond to these directions in similar ways?

claims.text: District educational guides are characteristically reluctant to specify exact national symbols, holidays, or historical figures to avoid impinging on teacher prerogatives.
claims.source_passage: These words disclose a characteristic reluctance on the part of district guides to impinge upon the teachers’ prerogatives—in this case, by not stating which national symbols, major holidays, historical figures, and historical events the local curriculum makers have in view.

claims.text: In the absence of specific content requirements, teachers do not respond to vague directions in similar ways, preventing shared knowledge among students entering the next grade.
claims.source_passage: But, in the absence of specifics, is there any reason to believe that different teachers will respond to these directions in similar ways? When children from diverse first-grade classes that follow these instructions enter second grade, what shared knowledge can the second-grade teacher take for granted among them?

claims.text: Even the highest-quality local curricula suffer from grave unevenness in specificity and coherence.
claims.source_passage: But even in this best of guides there is grave unevenness in specificity and coherence.

claims.text: The 'spiraling' method of curriculum design is universally experienced by students as boring repetition rather than deepening understanding.
claims.source_passage: The theory behind this sort of repetition is one of deepening through “spiraling.” But it is universally experienced by students as boring repetition, as in the oft-heard complaint from those who have been made to read Charlotte’s Web three times in six grades.

claims.text: The 'strand' approach to curriculum inevitably results in frequent repetitions and significant knowledge gaps.
claims.source_passage: Frequent repetitions and gaps are the besetting weaknesses of local curricula, and they are made inevitable when the strand approach is compounded with vagueness.

claims.text: It is an unwarranted assumption that individual teachers will fill the knowledge gaps left by vague local guidelines.
claims.source_passage: It might be assumed that the individual teacher would fill in these gaps. But experience has shown this to be an unwarranted assumption.

claims.text: Gaps in local curriculum guidelines directly result in gaps in students' cognitive development.
claims.source_passage: Major gaps in the local guidelines become major gaps in students’ minds.

claims.text: The degree of curricular ineptitude found in the United States is unique among developed nations.
claims.source_passage: How did we achieve this degree of curricular ineptitude, unique in the developed world?

claims.text: Contemporary conceptual curricula were developed starting in the 1930s as a progressive reaction against content-oriented approaches.
claims.source_passage: Beginning in the 1930s as part of the advance of progressive education in the public schools and the colleges of education, there were curriculum-revision movements across the land. Over the past six decades, such vague, gap-ridden “conceptual” curricula have been developed as a reaction to earlier, content-oriented approaches to forming a curriculum.

claims.text: Large-scale conceptual schemes and classifications used in curricular 'strands' are inherently arbitrary.
claims.source_passage: The first inherent weakness is the arbitrariness of the large-scale conceptual schemes and classifications that make up all such curricular “strands” or “objectives.”

claims.text: National scientific organizations produce conceptual schemes that are inconsistent with one another.
claims.source_passage: Equally striking is the arbitrariness of the various conceptual schemes recently produced by curricular experts for the American Association for the Advancement of Science, the National Council of Teachers of Science, and the National Academy of Sciences. These documents all follow different conceptual schemes.

claims.text: Curricula that rely primarily on processes, 'objectives,' and 'strands' suffer from fundamental weaknesses even in high-performing districts.
claims.source_passage: the examples just cited illustrate the kinds of defects that result from curricula which rely primarily on processes, “objectives” and “strands.” Even the best local guides of this type have fundamental weaknesses.

claims.text: Overreliance on large-scale abstract objectives fails to compel a definite or coherent sequence of instruction.
claims.source_passage: These general objectives do not compel either a definite or a coherent sequence of instruction.

claims.text: The relationship between broad conceptual schemes and specific classroom content is tenuous and uncertain.
claims.source_passage: That is because the large conceptual scheme and its concrete expressions (through particular contents) have a very tenuous and uncertain relationship to each other.

claims.text: General objectives like 'understand interactions of matter and energy' fail to provide teachers with practical guidance for content selection.
claims.source_passage: A big scheme is just too general to guide the teacher in the selection of particulars. For instance, one multigrade science objective in our superior local district states, “Understand interactions of matter and energy.” This is operationally equivalent to saying, “Understand physics, chemistry, and biology.”

claims.text: The lack of content specificity is the primary cause of repetitions and gaps in local curricula.
claims.source_passage: Apart from a misplaced reliance in local curricula on arbitrary conceptual schemes, the main source of their repetitions and gaps continues to be their lack of content specificity.

claims.text: American curricula are unevenly specific, often detailing foreign geography while omitting essential domestic geographical features.
claims.source_passage: How, then, may we explain the absence of similar specific guidance to the most important geographical features of the United States? ...Yet all the foregoing geographical detail about Asia and Africa is presented as essential knowledge.

claims.text: The omission of specific domestic content in curricula is motivated by the desire to avoid political or social controversy.
claims.source_passage: To select Asian geographical features is a noncontroversial activity. To select geographical features within the United States may be considered a highly controversial act.

claims.text: Textbooks contain too much detail to serve as a guide for what is essential knowledge in early grades.
claims.source_passage: The textbooks themselves contain far too much detail to be useful in that regard.

claims.text: Current language arts curricula in the U.S. are characterized by unhelpful vagueness and a lack of specific literary content.
claims.source_passage: Nowhere is this unhelpful vagueness more apparent than in language arts curricula throughout the United States. While the specificity of some district curricula in science and social studies may be admirable in places, the same cannot be said for any local language arts framework I know of.

claims.text: Language arts objectives often repeat the exact same 'spiraling' requirements from grade one through grade six, providing no specific content growth.
claims.source_passage: The following is the first broad “objective” to be found in the district’s language arts curriculum, and like the other language arts objectives, it spirals through each of the first six grades... Grade one: “Develop and apply a word attack system independently...” [repeated verbatim through Grade six]

claims.text: A purely formal approach to teaching literacy skills that lacks guidance to specific writings is psychologically unsound.
claims.source_passage: While these elements of literacy do need to be taught, this purely formal approach to teaching them, lacking as it does any guidance to specific writings through which they might be taught, is psychologically unsound, according to the research community.

claims.text: Process-focused curricula that neglect specific books and stories fail to guide teachers and miss the opportunity to teach skills and traditional knowledge simultaneously.
claims.source_passage: Its sharp focus on process, as well as its neglect of particular books, stories, and essays as means of teaching literacy skills, not only fails to guide teachers but misses an opportunity to teach both skills and important traditional knowledge simultaneously to children.

claims.text: Vague curricular frameworks lead to redundant readings of the same texts and contribute to disadvantaged children's lack of background knowledge.
claims.source_passage: It is precisely the sort of vague framework that leads to multiple readings of Charlotte’s Web, and to disadvantaged children’s lack of acquaintance with knowledge that advantaged children pick up at home.

claims.text: Detailed guidelines reduce the total amount of information a teacher must treat as essential by discriminating between required and desirable knowledge.
claims.source_passage: The selection of particular important facts actually reduces the total amount of specific information that a teacher needs to consider essential.

claims.text: School districts issue vague guidelines primarily to avoid the controversy associated with making specific content choices.
claims.source_passage: why be specific when vagueness will avoid controversy?

claims.text: Without specific content guidelines, disadvantaged students are forced into an unfair system where the rules of academic success are never defined.
claims.source_passage: On the other hand, without specifics, disadvantaged students and their teachers play a Kafkaesque game whose rules are never clearly defined. Soon the unlucky are consigned to slow tracks and never enter the mainstream of learning and society.

claims.text: Inequality in schools is primarily caused by inherent shortcomings in curricular organization rather than differences in innate ability or willingness to learn.
claims.source_passage: What chiefly makes our schools unfair, then, even for children who remain in the same school year after year, is that some students are learning less than others not because of their innate lack of academic ability or their lack of willingness to learn but because of inherent shortcomings in curricular organization.

claims.text: The major source of avoidable injustice in schools is the systemic failure to teach children the prerequisite knowledge needed for the next grade.
claims.source_passage: A systemic failure to teach all children the knowledge they need in order to understand what the next grade has to offer is the major source of avoidable injustice in our schools.

claims.text: Individual schools must prioritize ensuring all children gain the prior knowledge required for the subsequent grade level.
claims.source_passage: Under these circumstances, the most important single task of an individual school is to ensure that all children within that school gain the prior knowledge they will need at the next grade level.

claims.text: Leaving curriculum content to the discretion of individual classrooms results in systemic unfairness.
claims.source_passage: Since our system currently leaves that supremely important task to the vagaries of individual classrooms, the result is a systemically imposed unfairness even for students who remain in the same school.

claims.text: Curricular incoherence causes academic failure and emotional devastation for students who move between schools.
claims.source_passage: Add to these academic handicaps the emotional devastation of not understanding what other children are understanding, and add to avoidable academic problems the unavoidable ones of adjusting to a new group, and it is not hard to understand why newcomers fail to flourish in American schools.

claims.text: The United States has one of the highest mobility rates of all developed countries, with approximately one-fifth of all Americans relocating annually.
claims.source_passage: The United States has one of the highest mobility rates of all developed countries; annually, about one-fifth of all Americans move.

claims.text: Low-income families are the social group with the highest percentage of school changes, causing them to suffer disproportionately from curricular incoherence.
claims.source_passage: the social group with the greatest percentage of school changers is made up of low-income families who move for economic reasons, and one understands more fully why disadvantaged children suffer disproportionately from the curricular incoherence of the American educational system.

claims.text: Disadvantaged children suffer disproportionately from the curricular incoherence of the American educational system because low-income families move more frequently for economic reasons.
claims.source_passage: the social group with the greatest percentage of school changers is made up of low-income families who move for economic reasons, and one understands more fully why disadvantaged children suffer disproportionately from the curricular incoherence of the American educational system.

claims.text: High mobility among low-income parents guarantees that disadvantaged children are the most severely affected by the educational handicaps of changing schools.
claims.source_passage: This high mobility among low-income parents guarantees that the disadvantaged children who will be most severely affected by the educational handicaps of changing schools are the very ones who move most often.

claims.text: In a typical inner-city school, only about half the students who begin the school year in September remain until May.
claims.source_passage: In a typical inner-city school, only about half the students who start in September are still there in May.

claims.text: The concept of a 'local school' is a myth if it implies a stable population of students attending throughout the year.
claims.source_passage: The myth of the local curriculum can be matched by the myth of the local school—if one means by that term not just a building and a staff but also the students who attend it during the year.

claims.text: Student mobility is rarely discussed in school reform due to a self-imposed ideological taboo against challenging the principle of an autonomous local curriculum.
claims.source_passage: Student mobility is rarely mentioned in discussions of school reform—which says more about the self-imposed restrictions on our educational thinking than about the urgencies of our educational problems. Any challenge to the principle of an autonomous local curriculum is considered taboo.

claims.text: The mobility rate in some inner-city schools exceeds 100 percent, meaning the total number of transfers exceeds the total enrollment.
claims.source_passage: Some inner-city schools in New York City and elsewhere have mobility rates of over 100 percent. That is to say, the total number of students moving in and out during the year exceeds the total number of students attending the school.

claims.text: The adverse effects of school moves on achievement contribute significantly to the low overall achievement of the American educational system.
claims.source_passage: The adverse effects of these moves on educational achievement contribute significantly to the low achievement of our system as a whole.

claims.text: Migrating students face 'unthinkable' fragmentation of education because of the lack of curricular coherence even within single schools.
claims.source_passage: Given the curricular incoherence faced even by those who stay at the same school, the fragmentation of the education provided to frequently moving students approaches the unthinkable.

claims.text: School mobility alone is as potent a predictor of poor school performance as poverty or low parental education levels.
claims.source_passage: In an interview, Dr. Wood summarized his findings by saying that mobility alone 'is as potent a predictor [of poor school performance] as having parents who are not well educated, or parents who are poor.'

claims.text: The educational failures associated with student mobility are not inevitable but are consequences of the current American system's structure.
claims.source_passage: It is important, however, not to regard these authoritative findings as inevitable consequences of student mobility. The findings describe, rather, the consequences of mobility within the American system as it is currently constituted.

claims.text: Countries that utilize a nationwide core curriculum experience significantly fewer adverse educational effects from student mobility than the United States.
claims.source_passage: There is strong evidence that the adverse educational effects of student mobility are much greater in the United States than in countries that use a nationwide core curriculum.

claims.text: Common learning goals, curricula, and assessments alleviate the learning disabilities faced by poorly achieving children who move between districts.
claims.source_passage: common learning goals, curriculum, and assessment within states (or within an entire nation), moreover, also alleviate the grave learning disabilities faced by children, especially poorly achieving children who move from one district to another with different curricula, assessment, and goals.

claims.text: Migrant children are being pushed out of school by the whiplash effect of changing curricula as they move between schools.
claims.source_passage: They almost were being pushed out of school by the whiplash effect of changing curriculum as they changed schools.

claims.text: The notion that each community must have its own unique curriculum is both silly and tragic.
claims.source_passage: The notion that each community must have a curriculum all its own is not only silly, but tragic.

claims.text: A democracy requires common cultural elements so that citizens can discuss collective problems using common meanings.
claims.source_passage: The first, as we have already seen, is the need of a democracy for many common elements in the culture of all the people, to the end that the people may discuss collective problems in terms that will convey common meanings.

claims.text: Failure to have uniformity in school subjects and grade placement is a gross injustice to millions of mobile American school children.
claims.source_passage: Under these conditions, failure to have a goodly measure of uniformity in school subjects and grade placement is a gross injustice to at least ten million school children at the present time.

claims.text: Subjects like math, science, history, and geography should not be subjects of controversy regarding a common curriculum sequence.
claims.source_passage: at least in those areas like math and science and the basic facts of history and geography, which, unlike sex education, are not and should not be subjects of controversy.

claims.text: The principle of local autonomy must be weighed against the paramount principles of educational excellence and social fairness.
claims.source_passage: But against the principle of local autonomy must be weighed the paramount principles of educational excellence and social fairness.

claims.text: A strong majority of the American public recognizes curricular commonality as a necessary precondition for educational excellence and fairness.
claims.source_passage: According to a 1991 Gallup Poll, that majority clearly recognizes the desirability of curricular coherence and commonality as a necessary precondition of excellence and fairness in education, and favors it by a very wide margin.

claims.text: Educational experts continue to view curricular particularism as an absolute and inviolable principle, ignoring the will of the majority.
claims.source_passage: How long before the clear will of the majority is accepted by the ruling experts, who continue to view curricular particularism as an absolute and inviolable principle?

claims.text: A core curriculum is a necessary but not sufficient condition for producing uniformly good educational results across a nation.
claims.source_passage: I am not making the argument that a core curriculum alone is necessary and sufficient to produce uniformly good results but, rather, that it is a necessary condition for producing them

claims.text: The American public favors a standardized national curriculum and national achievement standards by a very wide margin.
claims.source_passage: a necessary precondition of excellence and fairness in education, and favors it by a very wide margin.

claims.text: Providing common intellectual capital in early grades is a necessary condition for producing excellence and fairness in education.
claims.source_passage: I am not making the argument that a core curriculum alone is necessary and sufficient to produce uniformly good results but, rather, that it is a necessary condition for producing them

claims.text: A core curriculum requires other factors, such as strong support in the general culture, to be sufficient for uniformly good results.
claims.source_passage: to which must be added other factors like strong support in the general culture.

claims.text: Under a fair educational system, achievement outcomes should reflect a normal distribution of innate ability.
claims.source_passage: If we assume that innate math ability is normally distributed, outcomes under a fair educational system should reflect that normal distribution.

claims.text: The Japanese educational system enables children with high math abilities to realize their talents while bringing low-ability students to acceptable minimal competence.
claims.source_passage: Children with high math abilities should be enabled to realize their talents, while the far greater number of children with middling abilities should achieve at a respectable level, and children with low abilities should be brought to an acceptable minimal competence. That pattern of equity and excellence is precisely the pattern illustrated in the Japanese results.

claims.text: The United States educational system produces an abnormally high percentage of students performing at very low levels of mathematical competence.
claims.source_passage: The graph shows that much larger percentages of U.S. students are performing at very low levels... the U.S. curve is abnormally populous on the left, low side of the scores

claims.text: The United States experienced an absolute educational decline in the period 1966–1980 according to domestic data.
claims.source_passage: The first is the evidence of an absolute educational decline in the United States in the period 1966–80 using U.S. data alone.

claims.text: The decline in American verbal SAT scores between 1966 and 1980 cannot be explained by the democratization of the test-taking population.
claims.source_passage: It is often stated that the reason for the sharp decline in verbal SAT scores in the United States between 1966 and 1980 was the democratization of the test-taking population. But, as I documented in 1987, this defense does not explain the 64 percent decline in the absolute number of high-achieving students

claims.text: A decline in the absolute number of top-performing students indicates a decline in the educational quality of the system for all students.
claims.source_passage: From the decline at the top, and the large drop in the overall mean, one can reasonably infer a decline in the educational quality of the system for all students, including those at the top.

claims.text: Longitudinal international comparisons are methodologically valid for indicating the direction of educational quality regardless of single-point comparison flaws.
claims.source_passage: As to the apples-vs.-oranges objection to international studies, it might apply to a single-shot comparison but not to identical comparisons over time. Whatever apple-orange inappropriatenesses existed in the first study would also exist in the second, making the direction of results highly informative.

claims.text: Between 1970 and the 1980s, the United States fell from seventh to fifteenth out of seventeen countries in science achievement.
claims.source_passage: In the 1970 study, the United States ranked seventh out of seventeen countries; in the 1980s it ranked fifteenth out of seventeen—third from the bottom

claims.text: The apples-vs.-oranges objection to international studies is invalid when applied to identical comparisons over time.
claims.source_passage: As to the apples-vs.-oranges objection to international studies, it might apply to a single-shot comparison but not to identical comparisons over time.

claims.text: The direction of change in international rankings reliably indicates the trajectory of a nation's educational quality compared to others.
claims.source_passage: Whatever apple-orange inappropriatenesses existed in the first study would also exist in the second, making the direction of results highly informative.

claims.text: International comparative data are not unfairly slanted against countries like the U.S. that educate the entire population because almost all modern nations educate over 98 percent of children in early grades.
claims.source_passage: Every modern nation educates over 98 percent of its children in the early grades, so the data are not unfairly slanted against a country like the United States, which “educates everybody.”

claims.text: Data derived from an entire range of population abilities indicate the inherent effectiveness of the educational system producing the results.
claims.source_passage: Also, because the comparative data are derived from the entire range of abilities in the population, they indicate the inherent effectiveness of the systems producing the results.

claims.text: Large-scale comparative research is more reliable than small-scale research because it washes out uncontrollable variables.
claims.source_passage: The special value of large-scale comparative research is that it washes out many of the variables which cannot be controlled for in the small scale.

claims.text: An educational arrangement can only be confidently accepted for general application if it succeeds on a large scale over a long period in diverse contexts.
claims.source_passage: Only when an educational arrangement succeeds on a large scale over a long period in diverse contexts can one confidently accept its claim to general application.

claims.text: Most educational programs work on a small scale regardless of quality due to the enthusiasm of participants.
claims.source_passage: Few school reforms have been tried that haven’t worked reasonably well on a small scale. Yet the primary cause of an initial success may not have been the innovation itself but other factors, such as the enthusiasm of the participants.

claims.text: Educational policies, like new drugs, cannot be securely evaluated until they have been used by thousands of people over many years.
claims.source_passage: no innovation can be securely evaluated right away, any more than a new drug can be confidently evaluated on the basis of small clinical trials.

claims.text: Between the 1970s and 1980s, science achievement rank in core-curriculum nations stayed stable or rose, while rank in noncore countries declined.
claims.source_passage: between the 1970s and the 1980s the rank order of science achievement in core-curriculum nations rose or stayed stable, while the rank order in noncore countries declined.

claims.text: Educational systems that rank high in fairness (equity) also rank high in excellence.
claims.source_passage: Strikingly, the systems that ranked high in fairness also ranked high in excellence; the best-performing systems were also the most equitable ones.

claims.text: The lack of a common core curriculum is the probable cause of high school-level underperformance in the Netherlands and Britain.
claims.source_passage: That 16 percent of Dutch schools fell below par was probably owing to the fact that in the Netherlands there are many schools doing their own thing—a theory strongly supported by the similar results from Britain, the other non-core-curriculum nation of Northern Europe.

claims.text: The Netherlands' science achievement fairness score, with 16 percent of schools below par, was significantly worse than its northern continental European neighbors.
claims.source_passage: Whereas Holland placed third of seventeen in average science achievement, its fairness score (16 percent of schools below par) was a distant last among the countries of northern continental Europe.

claims.text: Britain's poor fairness score (19 percent of schools below par) was likely caused by the lack of a common core curriculum at the time of the IEA study.
claims.source_passage: Britain did even worse, with 19 percent of schools below par. One cause of these results was probably that Holland and Britain, in contrast to their European neighbors, lacked a common core curriculum.

claims.text: A country without nationwide curricular standards can still achieve high results and fairness if school mobility is low and students receive a coherent local curriculum.
claims.source_passage: A country that lacks nationwide curricular standards can produce high average results when each of its schools has a good common curriculum and when mobility rates between schools are low, keeping the student body stable, so that each child receives a coherent education.

claims.text: The Swiss education system achieves excellence and fairness because low mobility rates preserve a coherent curriculum for children within individual cantons.
claims.source_passage: Switzerland, for example... uses a different core curriculum within each small canton. But since the Swiss do not move very often, the result is a coherent curriculum for each child.

claims.text: The absence of a core curriculum in the Netherlands permitted 'spots of unfairness' where students in inferior schools were being cheated despite a generally high-performing system.
claims.source_passage: All the more reason to conclude that, in the Dutch context, students at these inferior schools were being cheated. In the Netherlands, the absence of a core curriculum has permitted spots of unfairness even within a generally excellent system.

claims.text: France has successfully diminished the socially induced learning gap between advantaged and disadvantaged children over several decades.
claims.source_passage: The French have been keeping educational data on a national scale for many decades and can justly pride themselves on having greatly diminished the socially induced learning gap between advantaged and disadvantaged children.

claims.text: In France, the learning gap between advantaged and disadvantaged students decreases with each school grade.
claims.source_passage: Remarkably, in France the initial gap between advantaged and disadvantaged students, instead of widening steadily as in the United States, decreases with each school grade.

claims.text: National core curricula provide a school-based education that relies less on the undependable home curriculum to supply the prior knowledge needed for learning.
claims.source_passage: Such success in achieving fairness is explained at least in part by the fact that national systems which have core curricula are able to provide a school-based education which relies relatively less on the undependable home curriculum to supply the prior knowledge needed for learning in each grade.

claims.text: The negative effects of an overall decline in school quality are felt most strongly among the least privileged children.
claims.source_passage: All children, including those of the middle class, will be poorly educated, but the negative effects will be strongest among the least privileged.

claims.text: The struggle for equality of educational opportunity is the new civil rights frontier.
claims.source_passage: Since inferior education is today the primary cause of social and economic injustice, the struggle for equality of educational opportunity is in effect the new civil rights frontier.

claims.text: A core curriculum induces grade readiness for all children and thus enables all members of a classroom to learn.
claims.source_passage: A core curriculum induces grade readiness for all children and thus enables all members of a classroom to learn. When all children possess the prior knowledge they need for understanding new material, the teacher will spend far less time in boring reviews and in special coaching of those who are behind.

claims.text: No noncore system cited in the IEA report has managed to achieve educational fairness.
claims.source_passage: I showed, moreover, that no noncore system cited in the report has managed to achieve fairness.

claims.text: Equality of educational opportunity does not mean that all students will achieve identical high test scores due to differences in aptitude and effort.
claims.source_passage: Since some children are apter and harder-working than others, equality of educational opportunity does not mean that all students will make very high test scores.

claims.text: Moderately high average achievement across all schools in a nation is a roughly accurate index of national educational fairness.
claims.source_passage: Nonetheless, basic gaps in knowledge can be compensated for in the classroom, as the international data prove. It follows that a moderately high average achievement in all schools is a roughly accurate index to national educational fairness, whether the schools are in the inner city or the suburbs.

claims.text: No educational system that lacks a shared core curriculum has managed to achieve educational fairness.
claims.source_passage: no noncore system cited in the report has managed to achieve fairness.

claims.text: Overall school test scores serve as an indicative index of a nation's educational fairness.
claims.source_passage: It follows that a moderately high average achievement in all schools is a roughly accurate index to national educational fairness, whether the schools are in the inner city or the suburbs.

claims.text: Schools cannot entirely equalize opportunity because the home environment functions as a primary site of learning where students spend the majority of their time.
claims.source_passage: It is of course true that good schools in the inner city can never entirely equalize educational opportunity compared with schools in the suburbs, because the home is also a school, where students spend more time than in the official one.

claims.text: Classroom instruction can compensate for basic knowledge gaps found in disadvantaged homes.
claims.source_passage: Nonetheless, basic gaps in knowledge can be compensated for in the classroom, as the international data prove.

claims.text: The inequitable distribution of intellectual capital in U.S. schools causes the achievement gap to widen every year until it becomes unbridgeable by fourth grade.
claims.source_passage: In the United States, our schools’ inequitable distribution of intellectual capital causes the gap between academic haves and have-nots to grow wider in each successive early grade until, by fourth grade, it is usually unbridgeable.

claims.text: Good schools have stronger positive effects on disadvantaged students than on advantaged ones, while poor schools have stronger negative effects on them.
claims.source_passage: It is the finding that good schools have stronger positive effects and poor schools stronger negative ones on disadvantaged students. This may be denominated “Coleman’s Law.”

claims.text: The educational disadvantage present at kindergarten typically widens into a massive gap by grade four in the current U.S. public school system.
claims.source_passage: Since our public school system as a whole is not very good, a small educational disadvantage in kindergarten normally widens to a big learning gap by grade four

claims.text: Head Start fails to achieve long-term educational improvement or educational equity.
claims.source_passage: But just how complex the search for equal educational opportunity has become may be gauged from the fact that Head Start does not achieve educational improvement, much less educational equity.

claims.text: Head Start was originally designed to prioritize health, nutrition, and parent involvement over academic learning.
claims.source_passage: The health, nutrition, motivation, and self-confidence of poor children were deemed to be at least as important to their future well-being as their academic learning.

claims.text: The academic benefits of Head Start fade because the program lacks academic coherence and accountability for specific outcomes.
claims.source_passage: While it is usually beneficial for a time, it tends to lack academic coherence and is rarely accountable for specific academic outcomes.

claims.text: The academic 'fade-out' of Head Start gains is exacerbated by students entering a public school system that is also fragmented and lacks coherence.
claims.source_passage: When its graduates subsequently enter a program that also lacks academic coherence and is also unaccountable for specific academic outcomes, the children have left the frying pan for the fire.

claims.text: Head Start should be extended and its academic rigor, along with that of the subsequent grade levels, should be increased.
claims.source_passage: This is implicitly an argument for extending Head Start while at the same time improving its academic rigor and that of the schooling which follows.

claims.text: The public school system in the United States is incoherent and fragmented, which causes the early benefits of Head Start to disappear.
claims.source_passage: Children fortunate enough to attend Head Start must enter a public school system that is incoherent and fragmented. But this explanation of fade-out is only half the story.

claims.text: The 'fade-out' of Head Start benefits occurs because students move from an academically vague preschool to an equally incoherent grade school system.
claims.source_passage: When its graduates subsequently enter a program that also lacks academic coherence and is also unaccountable for specific academic outcomes, the children have left the frying pan for the fire.

claims.text: Preschool programs in other countries successfully achieve long-term academic benefits for disadvantaged students.
claims.source_passage: Preschool programs elsewhere in the world do achieve long-term academic benefits for disadvantaged students.

claims.text: In France, early schooling provides a permanent boost to the educational achievements of children from low-paid worker and immigrant families.
claims.source_passage: In France, early schooling permanently boosts educational achievements of children of low-paid workers and immigrants from North Africa.

claims.text: The durability of early education benefits in France is due to professional staffing and well-defined academic goals.
claims.source_passage: The école maternelle (attended by over 90 percent of French three- and four-year-olds) lasts all day and goes twelve months a year, is staffed by professionals, and has well-defined academic goals.

claims.text: American educational experts use resourceful techniques to deny the desirability of an academic core curriculum.
claims.source_passage: American experts have developed astonishingly resourceful techniques for denying the desirability of an academic core curriculum.

claims.text: A diverse and nomadic nation has a greater functional need for a grade-by-grade core curriculum than less migratory and diverse countries.
claims.source_passage: Indeed, if the foregoing analyses are right, a diverse, nomadic nation like ours has a greater need for a grade-by-grade core curriculum than do countries that are less migratory and diverse

claims.text: The educational needs of young children are essentially the same throughout the world.
claims.source_passage: the educational needs of young children have become very much the same throughout the world.

claims.text: It is a fundamental injustice for a child's school learning to be determined by the intellectual capital provided by their home.
claims.source_passage: It is a fundamental injustice that what American children are enabled to learn in school should be determined by what their homes have already given them.

claims.text: Advantaged American children are currently not receiving an adequate education in public schools.
claims.source_passage: nothing said here should be construed to imply that our advantaged children are on the whole receiving an adequate education. They are not.

claims.text: A lack of intellectual capital is a solvable educational problem rather than an unchangeable fate.
claims.source_passage: A child’s initial lack of intellectual capital is not an immutable given that our schools are powerless to change; rather, it is a challenge that schools can meet by overcoming their academic incoherence.

claims.text: The only proven way to achieve educational excellence and fairness is through definite skills and a solid core of content in each year of schooling.
claims.source_passage: Throughout the world, just one way has been devised to meet the double challenge of educational excellence and fairness: to teach definite skills and a solid core of content appropriate in an effective manner in each year of preschool and grade-school education.

claims.text: John Dewey’s educational writings are characterized by internal inconsistencies that complicate their use as a definitive foundation for democratic education.
claims.source_passage: In the early years of this century, John Dewey (who said many, sometimes inconsistent things about education) reaffirmed the connections between such common learnings...

claims.text: The theory that intellectual competencies are independent of content is an inherently attractive and tempting idea for educators, even though it is empirically false.
claims.source_passage: The claim that all-purpose intellectual competencies are independent of the matter out of which they have been formed, if it corresponded to reality, would indeed be an attractive educational idea.

claims.text: History education specifically enables citizens to recognize and defeat political ambition by providing them with the expertise to judge the actions and designs of men.
claims.source_passage: It will qualify them as judges of the actions and designs of men; it will enable them to know ambition under every disguise it may assume; and knowing it to defeat its views.

claims.text: The educational 'tool metaphor' treats cognitive skills as if they were universal mechanical instruments, like 'pliers and wrenches,' that function independently of context.
claims.source_passage: The chief aims of education would simply be to ensure that children acquired 'love of learning' and gained 'critical-thinking' techniques for acquiring and using whatever they would need later—the pliers and wrenches that would permit them to set up shop even on a desert island.

claims.text: The local control of education is a democratic virtue and desirable principle, but it becomes secondary when it conflicts with the principles of excellence and social fairness.
claims.source_passage: The principle of the local curriculum is desirable in a democracy, so long as schooling is effective and fair. But against the principle of local autonomy must be weighed the paramount principles of educational excellence and social fairness.

claims.text: Curriculum makers find the selection of foreign geographical features (e.g., Asian rivers) to be noncontroversial, whereas selecting domestic features is avoided to prevent local political conflict.
claims.source_passage: To select Asian geographical features is a noncontroversial activity. To select geographical features within the United States may be considered a highly controversial act.

claims.text: The 'language instinct' identified by Steven Pinker suggests that human curiosity and the drive to learn are inborn biological traits that must be 'systematically thwarted' to produce boredom.
claims.source_passage: Their natural curiosity and eagerness are inborn instincts that have to be systematically thwarted in order to make the children bored or indifferent.

claims.text: Multi-year curriculum goals are structurally ineffective because the primary unit of accountability and change in schooling is the single school year and the individual teacher.
claims.source_passage: I emphasize in this context the importance of defining content for a particular grade level, since the school year is the critical unit for curricular planning. The normal pattern is: a new year, a new teacher. Guidelines developed in multiyear units are ineffective in practice because students change teachers in successive years.

evidence.description: Thomas Jefferson's proposed bill for the general diffusion of knowledge in Virginia.
evidence.source_reference: Jefferson, The Life and Selected Writings of Thomas Jefferson
evidence.source_passage: The need in a democracy to teach children a shared body of knowledge was explained many years ago by Thomas Jefferson when he described his bill “to diffuse knowledge more generally through the mass of the people.”

evidence.description: The 1994 unanimous ratification by the Parliament of Norway regarding a core curriculum based on shared frames of reference.
evidence.source_reference: Hernes, Core Curriculum for Primary, Secondary, and Adult Education in Norway
evidence.source_passage: the dependency of the democratic vision on commonly shared knowledge was brought up to date and ratified unanimously by the Parliament of Norway

evidence.description: Expert testimony from George Kennan regarding the durability of Russian democracy based on literacy.
evidence.source_reference: George Kennan, TV interview
evidence.source_passage: Kennan predicted that the democratic revolution would prove more lasting than its predecessor: the Soviet state had been forced by economic imperatives to make its people literate

evidence.description: Pierre Bourdieu's sociological research showing that 'cultural capital' correlates with wealth, status, and the acquisition of more abilities.
evidence.source_reference: Bourdieu, 'Le Capital social: notes provisoires'

evidence.description: The Biblical paradox of Matthew 13:12 used as a structural principle for educational outcomes (the Matthew Effect).
evidence.source_reference: Walberg and Tsai, 'Matthew Effects in Education'

evidence.description: Cross-national statistical data (Belgium, France, Germany, etc.) showing that educational achievement is possible for heterogeneous societies.
evidence.source_reference: Centre for Educational Research and Innovation, 'Immigrants’ Children at School'

evidence.description: French studies showing that the performance gap between Third World immigrant students and native French students decreased markedly due to standardized schooling.
evidence.source_reference: Boulot and Boyzon-Fradet, 'Les Immigrés et l’école'

evidence.description: The Coleman Report found that the home environment is the primary determinant of academic success in the mid-1960s US education system.
evidence.source_reference: Coleman, Equality of Educational Opportunity (1966)
evidence.source_passage: In 1966, the Coleman Report found that under the current American educational system the home is... the decisive influence on academic outcomes

evidence.description: Analysis of Army Alpha tests from WWI showing a .72 correlation between soldier IQ and the educational quality of their home state.
evidence.source_reference: Bagley, Determinism in Education (1925)
evidence.source_passage: Bagley found a high correlation, .72, between the mean IQ of soldiers and the educational quality of their native states during the period when the soldiers would have been in grade school.

evidence.description: Data showing African-American soldiers from states with high-quality school systems had higher IQs than white soldiers from states with poor school systems in 1925.
evidence.source_reference: Bagley, Determinism in Education (1925)
evidence.source_passage: Interestingly, African-American soldiers from educationally high-ranking states had higher IQs than white soldiers from low-ranking states.

evidence.description: The 'horseless carriage' metaphor for early automobiles as an example of cognitive accommodation.
evidence.source_reference: None cited
evidence.source_passage: When the automobile first came on the scene, people called it a “horseless carriage,” thus accommodating the new to the old.

evidence.description: The analogy of the solar system being used to explain the structure of an atom.
evidence.source_reference: None cited
evidence.source_passage: When a teacher tells a class that electrons go around the nucleus of an atom as the planets go around the sun, that analogy may be helpful for students who already know about the solar system, but not for students who don’t.

evidence.description: The analogy of the 'horseless carriage' demonstrating how new technology was understood via old concepts.
The 1989 education summit involving President Bush and 49 governors establishing 'ready to learn' as a priority.
Stevenson and Stigler's research in 'The Learning Gap' regarding commonality of preparation in classrooms.
evidence.source_reference: Stevenson and Stigler, The Learning Gap (1992)

evidence.description: Comparative study showing the widening gap in math achievement between Chinese, Japanese, and American students from 6th to 11th grade.
evidence.source_reference: Stevenson, Chuansheng, and Shin-Ying (1993)

evidence.description: Stevenson and Stigler found that the primary challenge for teaching is variability in educational preparation, and the gap between American and Asian students widens from 6th to 11th grade.
evidence.source_reference: Stevenson and Stigler, The Learning Gap (1992)
evidence.source_passage: The learning gap that Stevenson and Stigler describe is a gap in academic performance between American and Asian students. Subsequent work by Stevenson and his colleagues has shown that this gap grows wider over time...

evidence.description: Constance Jones found that providing children with background preparation led to a sharp decline in absenteeism and disciplinary problems.
evidence.source_reference: Constance Jones, Quality and Equity of Education Outcomes
evidence.source_passage: Constance Jones has found that when American grade-schoolers are offered schooling that provides them with needed background preparation, there is a sharp decline in absenteeism and disciplinary problems.

evidence.description: Dennis O'Keeffe concluded that students equipped with intellectual tools show lower levels of truancy.
evidence.source_reference: Dennis O'Keeffe, Truancy in English Secondary Schools
evidence.source_passage: In Britain, research by Dennis O’Keeffe has similarly concluded that if students are “equipped with the requisite intellectual tools, levels of truancy are less.”

evidence.description: Steven Pinker demonstrated that children are born with a language instinct.
evidence.source_reference: Steven Pinker, The Language Instinct
evidence.source_passage: Steven Pinker has shown that children are born with a “language instinct.”

evidence.description: Data showing library use typically rises by 70 percent when a content-rich curriculum is introduced.
evidence.source_reference: Not explicitly named, cited as typical occurrences
evidence.source_passage: In the first year during which a coherent, content-oriented curriculum is introduced into an American elementary school, library use rises typically by 70 percent.

evidence.description: A district superintendent's realization that no principal in his district could identify minimal grade-level content.
A letter from a mother of identical twins in the same school who were learning 'totally different things' in different classes.
Research showing American teachers frequently omit topics from textbooks, leading to cumulative knowledge gaps.
evidence.source_reference: Stevenson and Stigler, The Learning Gap, 1992
evidence.source_passage: American teachers often omit some topics. Different topics are omitted by different teachers, thereby making it impossible for the children’s later teachers to know what has been covered at earlier grades

evidence.description: The experience of a Core Knowledge school where district guidelines were so vague that a specific curriculum could be implemented without technically 'giving up' any existing district content.
Analysis of first-grade social studies instructions that use words like 'historical figures' and 'specific groups' without naming them.
Vague district guidelines requiring students to 'Identify beliefs and value systems of specific groups' without naming the groups.
A 'superior' local guide that includes specific requirements like using the term 'decay' and locating the North and South Poles.
The 'Seeds' curriculum spiral showing nearly identical requirements for plants/seeds across grades 1, 2, 4, and 5.
Specific missing topics in a high-quality local curriculum including photosynthesis, simple tools, and standard units of measurement.
Historical curriculum-revision movements cited in Diane Ravitch's work.
evidence.source_reference: Ravitch, The Troubled Crusade, 52–55

evidence.description: A comparison of divergent conceptual schemes for science education produced by the AAAS, NCTS, and National Academy of Sciences.

A social science curriculum guide from a 'superior' local district that requires specific knowledge of Asian and African geography (e.g., Mekong River, Hwang River) but fails to mention major U.S. rivers or mountains.
A language arts curriculum from a 'superior' local district that repeats the identical 'word attack system' objective for every grade level from 1 to 6.
National Academy of Education report stating that purely formal approaches to reading are unsound.
evidence.source_reference: National Academy of Education, Becoming a Nation of Readers

evidence.description: United States General Accounting Office data on the high mobility rate of Americans and its impact on school children.
evidence.source_reference: General Accounting Office, Elementary School Children

evidence.description: United States General Accounting Office report showing one-fifth of Americans relocate annually and the disruption it causes children.
evidence.source_reference: General Accounting Office, Elementary School Children, 1
evidence.source_passage: According to the United States General Accounting Office, about one fifth of all Americans relocate every year: 'The United States has one of the highest mobility rates of all developed countries; annually, about one-fifth of all Americans move.'
