evidence.description: Data showing the average student transfer rate in Milwaukee public elementary schools is approximately 30 percent.
evidence.source_reference: Cohen, 'Moving Images'
evidence.source_passage: The average rate for Milwaukee public elementary schools is around 30 percent.

evidence.description: The Hawthorne School (P.S. 179) in San Antonio report showing a mobility rate of 54.4 percent among a 522-student population.
evidence.source_reference: Public School No. 179, official reports
evidence.source_passage: On the first page of the report, under the rubric 'Students,' was a box with the following data... Percent of membership transferred: 54.4%

evidence.description: GAO report finding that 1/6 of all 3rd graders and 1/4 of low-income 3rd graders have attended 3+ schools.
evidence.source_reference: General Accounting Office, Elementary School Children, 5
evidence.source_passage: A recent report from the General Accounting Office stated that one sixth of all third graders attend at least three schools between first and third grade. A quarter of low-income third graders have attended at least three different schools.

evidence.description: Study of 9,915 children by Dr. David Wood that isolated mobility from other variables like poverty and race.
evidence.source_reference: Wood, Halfon, and Scarlata, 1993
evidence.source_passage: Dr. David Wood and his associates have analyzed the effects of mobility on 9,915 children. With this large group, the researchers were able to factor out the influences of poverty, race, single-parent households, and lack of parental education in order to isolate just the effects of school changing alone.

evidence.description: Research summary by Herbert Walberg citing Bruce Straits regarding the role of common curricula in alleviating mobility issues.
evidence.source_reference: Walberg, 'Improving Local Control and Learning'; Straits, 'Residence, Migration, and School Progress'
evidence.source_passage: In a summary of research, Herbert Walberg, citing the work of Bruce C. Straits, states that 'common learning goals, curriculum, and assessment within states (or within an entire nation), moreover, also alleviate the grave learning disabilities faced by children...'

evidence.description: Wood's 1993 results regarding the adverse educational effects of student mobility in the United States context.
evidence.source_reference: Wood, Halfon, and Scarlata (1993)

evidence.description: Research summary stating that common learning goals and assessments alleviate learning disabilities for mobile children.
evidence.source_reference: Herbert Walberg, citing Bruce C. Straits

evidence.description: A study suggesting a national curriculum for migrant children to combat the whiplash effect of changing schools.
evidence.source_reference: Education Week (November 29, 1989)

evidence.description: A 1991 Gallup Poll showing 68% to 81% favorability for a national curriculum or achievement standards.
evidence.source_reference: Elam, Rose, and Gallup (1991)

evidence.description: Cross-national comparison of math achievement among 11th graders in Japan and the US, controlling for socioeconomic sampling and testing conditions.
evidence.source_reference: Harold Stevenson and colleagues
evidence.source_passage: In conducting this cross-national comparison, Harold Stevenson and his colleagues made sure that the test accurately reflected the knowledge and skills which eleventh graders in both nations were mandated to learn, and that the socioeconomic sampling of students was exactly comparable.

evidence.description: College Board data showing a 64 percent decline in the absolute number of students scoring above 600 on the verbal SAT.
evidence.source_reference: College Board, 'College-Bound Seniors'
evidence.source_passage: With a constant number of about one million students taking the test, the percentage of students who scored over 600 was 7.3 percent in 1984 and 11.4 percent in 1972.

evidence.description: IEA multinational study of science achievement ranking the US 7th of 17 in 1970 and 15th of 17 in the 1980s.
evidence.source_reference: International Association for the Evaluation of Educational Achievement (IEA)
evidence.source_passage: In the 1970 study, the United States ranked seventh out of seventeen countries; in the 1980s it ranked fifteenth out of seventeen—third from the bottom

evidence.description: Comparison of rank movement between core nations (Sweden, Finland, Japan) and noncore nations (England, Australia, U.S.) in IEA science studies.
evidence.source_reference: International Association for the Evaluation of Educational Achievement (1988)
evidence.source_passage: Among the three noncore countries, England dropped from ninth to eleventh, Australia from third to tenth, and the United States from seventh to fifteenth.

evidence.description: Data showing percentages of schools with below-standard average achievement in core (Japan 1%, Hungary 0%) vs noncore nations (U.S. 30%, England 19%).
evidence.source_reference: International Association for the Evaluation of Educational Achievement (1988)
evidence.source_passage: Among the Finnish schools, only 2 percent of schools showed below-standard average achievement... Among the noncore countries, the percentages of schools below par were: Australia, 8 percent; the Netherlands, 16 percent; England, 19 percent; and the United States, 30 percent.

evidence.description: The thalidomide affair used as an analogy for the long-term risk of unvetted policies/innovations.
evidence.source_reference: None cited
evidence.source_passage: as both the thalidomide affair and U.S. education since the late 1960s have dramatically illustrated.

evidence.description: IEA study data on science achievement showing the Netherlands placed 3rd of 17 in average achievement but last in Northern Europe for fairness.
evidence.source_reference: International Association for the Evaluation of Educational Achievement, Science Achievement in Seventeen Countries

evidence.description: Comparative studies showing Switzerland achieves high excellence and fairness despite lacking a national core curriculum.
evidence.source_reference: Lapointe, Mead, and Askew, Learning Mathematics and Learning Science

evidence.description: French longitudinal data showing children of North African immigrants narrowing the learning gap by seventh grade after attending preschool.
evidence.source_reference: Boulot and Boyzon-Fradet, Les Immigres et l’ecole; and CERI, Immigrants’ Children at School

evidence.description: IEA data analysis demonstrating that all nations rated as 'fair' used a core curriculum approach.
evidence.source_reference: International Association for the Evaluation of Educational Achievement, Science Achievement in Seventeen Countries

evidence.description: IEA report on Science Achievement in Seventeen Countries showing lack of fairness in noncore systems.
evidence.source_reference: International Association for the Evaluation of Educational Achievement (1988)

evidence.description: Longitudinal tracking of acquired learning abilities from grade one to grade four showing a widening gap.
evidence.source_reference: Walter Loban (1960s)

evidence.description: The 1966 report on equality of educational opportunity showing school impact on disadvantaged children.
evidence.source_reference: Coleman Report (1966)

evidence.description: A comprehensive review of research on Head Start effects showing test scores of graduates do not remain superior to non-graduates in the long run.
evidence.source_reference: U.S. Department of Health and Human Services (1985)

evidence.description: An anecdote about HEW officials' preference for 'Sesame Street' over Head Start to illustrate non-academic priorities.
evidence.source_reference: Edward Zigler, Head Start (1992)

evidence.description: Comparison of US Head Start (3 hours, non-professional, non-academic) versus French preschool (all day, professional, well-defined academic goals).
Long-term data from France showing early schooling permanently boosts achievements for children of low-paid workers and North African immigrants.
evidence.source_reference: Boulot and Boyzon-Fradet, Les Immigrés et l’école; Centre for Educational Research and Innovation
evidence.source_passage: In France, early schooling permanently boosts educational achievements of children of low-paid workers and immigrants from North Africa.

evidence.description: Research implying that while Head Start provides momentary benefits, the incoherent public school system erases them.
evidence.source_reference: U.S. Department of Health and Human Services, The Impact of Head Start on Children, Families and Communities (1985)
evidence.source_passage: The researchers imply that while Head Start may have yielded momentary benefits, later schooling erases them.

concepts.author_definition: Common background information taken for granted in public discourse that allows members of a community to communicate and interpret new ideas without constant extra explanation.
A sociological concept expressing the socioeconomic value of the knowledge and skills acquired through schooling, necessary for participation in the modern workplace.
An educational institution designed to provide all children equally with a shared body of knowledge to ensure social community and economic independence.
The cumulative advantage where those who start with knowledge/capital gain more, while those who lack it fall further behind.
concepts.source_passage: “For whosoever hath, to him shall be given, and he shall have more abundance: but whosoever hath not, from him shall be taken away even that he hath.”

concepts.author_definition: Knowledge that is widely useful and negotiable across society, enabling communication between different members of a democracy.
concepts.source_passage: not just any knowledge will work—only that knowledge which constitutes the shared intellectual currency of the society.

concepts.author_definition: The belief that education should provide content-neutral skills (critical thinking, love of learning) rather than specific factual knowledge.
concepts.common_alternative: The dominant progressive educational theory for the last 60 years.
concepts.source_passage: American educational theory has held that the child needs to be given the all-purpose tools that are needed for him or her to continue learning and adapting.

concepts.author_definition: The psychological process of learning something new by connecting it to and fitting it within previously known information.
concepts.source_passage: Psychological research has shown that the ability to learn something new depends on an ability to accommodate the new thing to the already known.

concepts.author_definition: A stock of potential analogies that enable new ideas to be assimilated to the already known.
The state of sharing critical skills, elements of knowledge, and vocabulary with other members of a specific grade-level community.
concepts.common_alternative: General developmental maturity or social-emotional readiness.

concepts.author_definition: The widening disparity in academic performance between students (or nations) that increases over time due to differences in initial preparation.
Knowledge that provides the mental 'hooks' or 'tentacles' necessary to grasp new information and fosters active curiosity.
A metaphor for the way background knowledge drives a child to actively seek out and grasp new, related information, as opposed to passive reception.
A biological drive, extending from the language instinct, that impels children to learn and join the adult community for survival.
The lack of a coherent, agreed-upon plan for teaching specific content grade-by-grade within American schools and districts.

concepts.term: The Myth of the Local Curriculum
concepts.author_definition: The false assumption that local control of schools results in a specific, agreed-upon set of content that children learn at each grade level.
Voluminous official documents produced by states and districts that use vague language to describe learning goals without actually specifying content.
A method of curriculum design where a few general objectives are repeated across several grades with the theoretical intent of deepening understanding.
concepts.common_alternative: A pedagogical technique to reinforce learning over time.
concepts.source_passage: The theory behind this sort of repetition is one of deepening through “spiraling.” But it is universally experienced by students as boring repetition.

concepts.author_definition: Broad, general principles or 'objectives' used as the primary organizational framework for a curriculum instead of specific content.
Curricula that attempt to gain unity and depth by following broad instructional aims and processes while avoiding the 'rote learning' of 'mere facts.'
concepts.source_passage: The new curricula have attempted to get beyond the “rote learning” of “mere facts,” and to gain unity and conceptual depth by following broad and deep instructional aims.

concepts.author_definition: The precise identification of the actual facts, places, and works that students are expected to learn, as opposed to abstract skill descriptions.
A method of instruction focused on process and abstract skills (like 'word attack clues') rather than engagement with specific, substantive writings.
A situation where the rules of success are never clearly defined for the participants, leading to arbitrary outcomes and the marginalization of the 'unlucky.'
The lack of a standardized, grade-by-grade content sequence that ensures students across different classrooms or schools share a common foundation of knowledge.
The percentage of students who transfer into and out of a school during the academic year relative to the total membership.
concepts.source_passage: The term of art for the percentages of transferred students is 'mobility rate.'

concepts.author_definition: The assumption that a school consists of a stable group of students who attend consistently from the beginning to the end of a year.
concepts.common_alternative: A school defined as a physical building or administrative staff.
concepts.source_passage: The myth of the local curriculum can be matched by the myth of the local school—if one means by that term not just a building and a staff but also the students who attend it during the year.

concepts.author_definition: The educational principle that school districts or individual schools should have the right to determine their own specific content independent of a national standard.
concepts.source_passage: Any challenge to the principle of an autonomous local curriculum is considered taboo.

concepts.author_definition: The educational frustration and failure caused by students frequently changing schools and encountering disconnected or contradictory curricula.
The principle that each local community must have its own unique curriculum, viewed by 'ruling experts' as an inviolable absolute.
Shared knowledge and meanings that allow participants in a democracy to discuss collective problems effectively.
An ideological assumption that the United States is fundamentally different from other nations, used as a defense to dismiss unfavorable international comparisons.
concepts.common_alternative: The idea that America has a unique national character and destiny.
concepts.source_passage: A long tradition of American exceptionalism assumes that the United States will always be an apple, while other nations will always be oranges.

concepts.author_definition: A statistical pattern where student achievement reflects the bell curve of innate ability, indicating a fair and effective educational system.
concepts.source_passage: If we assume that innate math ability is normally distributed, outcomes under a fair educational system should reflect that normal distribution.

concepts.author_definition: A sociological phenomenon where an innovation appears successful primarily due to the enthusiasm of participants rather than the innovation's inherent quality.
concepts.source_passage: This false positive is known among sociologists as the “Hawthorne effect,” and its workings are often invoked when a school reform that one doesn’t like appears to be successful.

concepts.author_definition: A measure of the extent to which a nation educates children at all schools to an appropriate average level of achievement, regardless of location or social class.
concepts.source_passage: This was a measure of the extent to which a nation educates children at all schools to an appropriate average level of achievement, regardless of location or social class.

concepts.author_definition: Nations that utilize a common national or shared curriculum across all schools, resulting in stable or rising international achievement ranks.
concepts.source_passage: the rank order of science achievement in core-curriculum nations rose or stayed stable, while the rank order in noncore countries declined.

concepts.author_definition: A metric used in the IEA studies based on the percentage of a nation's schools falling below a certain achievement threshold; an index of educational equity.
The variable and often insufficient body of knowledge provided to children at home, which disadvantaged children cannot rely on for school success.
The contemporary struggle for equality of educational opportunity, characterized as the primary modern mechanism for social and economic justice.
The state in which every child in a classroom possesses the necessary prior knowledge to understand and learn new material being taught.

The informal education provided by the family environment, which the author argues is a primary driver of educational advantage or disadvantage because children spend more time there than in formal schools.
The principle that the quality of a school (good or poor) has a disproportionately large impact—either positive or negative—on disadvantaged students compared to advantaged ones.
The phenomenon where the academic and cognitive benefits of early intervention programs (specifically Head Start) dissipate shortly after the student transitions to the standard public school system.
The French preschool system, characterized by universal attendance (90%+), professional staffing, and well-defined academic goals.
The state of a school system that lacks a shared core of content and standards, leading to fragmented learning, especially for mobile students.
A description of the United States as a country with high rates of internal migration and diverse student populations.
A formal, process-oriented approach to literacy that emphasizes phonetic, structural, and contextual clues over specific reading content.
The pattern identified in the Coleman Report where academic outcomes are primarily determined by home environment rather than school quality.
A metaphor for specific, widely useful, and shared intellectual currency that allows for effective communication across a whole society.
Historical educational shifts beginning in the 1930s that replaced content-oriented approaches with vague, process-based guidelines.
Social inequalities that arise not from innate ability but from the unequal distribution of shared cultural knowledge.

cases.name: 1994 Norway Core Curriculum Reform
cases.description: The Norwegian Parliament unanimously ratified a core curriculum centered on the principle that shared knowledge is essential for 'popular enlightenment' and to prevent social inequality that 'undemocratic forces' could exploit.
key_passages: In 1994, the dependency of the democratic vision on commonly shared knowledge was brought up to date and ratified unanimously by the Parliament of Norway
Common background knowledge is thus at the core of a national network of communication between members of a democratic community.

cases.name: The Soviet/Russian Transition
cases.description: According to George Kennan, the Soviet regime's economic need for a literate workforce created a populace that was less malleable and more capable of sustaining a democratic revolution than the uneducated peasantry of 1917.
key_passages: the Soviet state had been forced by economic imperatives to make its people literate, whereas the revolution of 1917 had been carried out by uneducated and therefore malleable peasants.

cases.name: The Soviet State
cases.description: The author contrasts the 1917 revolution (uneducated peasants) with the modern Soviet state, which was forced by economic necessity to produce a literate populace, making its democratic revolution potentially more durable.
key_passages: the Soviet state had been forced by economic imperatives to make its people literate, whereas the revolution of 1917 had been carried out by uneducated and therefore malleable peasants.

cases.name: The French School System
cases.description: Cited as an example of a nation with high heterogeneity (immigrant populations) that successfully narrowed performance gaps through a common curriculum.
key_passages: The French studies have been the fullest. They show that, when all other factors are accounted for, the difference in performance between Third World immigrant students and French students 'decreased more or less markedly'.

cases.name: US Schools 1960s-1990s
cases.description: A period dominated by 'process-oriented' educational theory where schools failed to mitigate the effects of home background on student achievement, leading to social determinism.
key_passages: In 1966, the Coleman Report found that under the current American educational system the home is... the decisive influence on academic outcomes
This pattern of social determinism found by Coleman in 1966 still persists in the schools of the 1990s.
contestable_aspects: The degree to which 'process-orientation' alone caused the lack of social mobility.
The interpretation of the Coleman Report as a result of curriculum rather than broader socio-economic factors.

cases.name: American vs. Asian Classrooms
cases.description: A comparison showing that American classrooms lack commonality of academic preparation, leading to slower progress and a widening achievement gap compared to Asian systems which achieve commonality.
key_passages: the progress of learning will be slow compared with that of educational systems which do achieve commonality of academic preparation
this gap grows wider over time, putting American students much further behind their Asian peers by eleventh grade than they were in sixth

cases.name: The Funnel-Shaped Gap
cases.description: The author compares the international achievement gap between US and Asian students with the internal gap between advantaged and disadvantaged US students. Both gaps widen progressively as students move through higher grades (e.g., from 6th to 11th).
key_passages: The funnel shape of this widening international gap has an eerie similarity to the funnel shape of the widening gap inside American schools between advantaged and disadvantaged students as they progress through the grades.

cases.name: The Core Knowledge School and the WSJ Reporter
cases.description: A Wall Street Journal reporter interviewed a principal at a new Core Knowledge school. The reporter expected the school to have 'given up' parts of the old curriculum to make room for the new, specific content. The principal explained that because the district guidelines were so vague, the specific Core Knowledge content could be added without removing anything, as the 'old curriculum' essentially didn't occupy any specific intellectual space.
key_passages: The skeptical reporter insistently repeated the question; he could not understand how two different curricula could occupy the same space at the same time. But of course the principal was right. The district guidelines were so vague that they and the more specific curriculum could both be followed at the same time.

cases.name: The Superior Local Guide
cases.description: An unnamed local district curriculum that Hirsch identifies as the best he has found. While it contains admirable specificities (e.g., North and South Poles, organic decay), it still falls victim to 'spiraling' defects, repeating units on seeds, the sun, and the moon across multiple grades while leaving out fundamental concepts like photosynthesis and measurement units.
key_passages: Let me therefore examine the very best one I have found so far, since it will, with fairness, illustrate the sort of problem that leads even in the best of cases to our curricular incoherence.
But even in this best of guides there is grave unevenness in specificity and coherence.

There was no indication even in this topflight local curriculum that children were to be introduced to the basic character of photosynthesis, nor that they were to be made aware of simple tools and how they work

cases.name: Superior Local District Curriculum
cases.description: A specific but unnamed school district's curriculum guide used as a representative case. It shows high specificity in foreign geography (identifying Mount Fuji and the Yangtze) but a total lack of specificity regarding U.S. geography and language arts content.
key_passages: First graders must “distinguish between a globe, political, and relief maps.”... By the time students get to grade six, they are to identify the Hwang, Yangtze, and Hsi rivers...

How, then, may we explain the absence of similar specific guidance to the most important geographical features of the United States? No rivers, not the Mississippi, the Missouri, the Snake, the Rio Grande, or the James...

cases.name: The plight of Jane
cases.description: A hypothetical second grader who moves to a new school. In her old school, history was deferred, but in the new school, classmates have already studied ancient Egypt. Consequently, she cannot understand the teacher's references to the Nile or Pyramids, leading to a cycle of academic and emotional failure.
key_passages: The new teacher’s references to the Nile River, the Pyramids, and hieroglyphics simply mystify Jane, and fail to convey to her the new information that the allusions were meant to impart.
Add to these academic handicaps the emotional devastation of not understanding what other children are understanding

cases.name: Public School No. 179 (Hawthorne School), San Antonio
cases.description: A PK-5 school with 522 students and a 54.4% mobility rate, serving a predominantly Hispanic population (83.2%). This case alerted Hirsch to the magnitude of the mobility problem.
key_passages: I was first alerted to the magnitude of the mobility problem in 1992 when I began receiving official reports from Core Knowledge Schools. The first one I saw was from Public School No. 179, in San Antonio, Texas, the Hawthorne School.
Transfers: In 108, Out 176. Percent of membership transferred: 54.4%

cases.name: New York City Inner-City Schools
cases.description: Schools cited as having mobility rates exceeding 100 percent, where the student body turns over completely or more during a single year.
key_passages: Some inner-city schools in New York City and elsewhere have mobility rates of over 100 percent. That is to say, the total number of students moving in and out during the year exceeds the total number of students attending the school.

cases.name: Children of migrant farm workers
cases.description: A demographic that moves several times a year following crop cycles. Hirsch highlights them as the extreme case demonstrating the need for a national core curriculum to provide educational coherence and stability against 'whiplash' effects.
key_passages: the issue had been inadequately dealt with for children of migrant farm workers, who must change schools several times in the course of the year

establishment of a national curriculum and diploma tailored to the needs of migrant children. 'In talking to the kids there were incredible levels of frustration,' he said. 'They almost were being pushed out of school by the whiplash effect of changing curriculum as they changed schools.'

cases.name: Japan (Math Achievement)
cases.description: Japan is presented as a 'core system' where math achievement follows a normal distribution. High-ability students realize their potential, while low-ability students reach acceptable competence, resulting in both excellence and equity.
key_passages: The graph shows that student math achievement in a core system (Japan) and in a noncore system (United States).
That pattern of equity and excellence is precisely the pattern illustrated in the Japanese results.
contestable_aspects: Whether cultural factors in Japan (like parental involvement or after-school tutoring) are more responsible than the curriculum itself.

cases.name: United States (Educational Decline 1966-1980)
cases.description: The US is characterized as a 'noncore system' that experienced absolute and relative decline. Achievement is skewed toward the low end, indicating a failure to help students reach their potential across the ability spectrum.
key_passages: The U.S. curve is abnormally populous on the left, low side of the scores, showing that large percentages of students who began with a normal diversity of math abilities have been hindered from achieving their potentials
the evidence of an absolute educational decline in the United States in the period 1966–80 using U.S. data alone.
contestable_aspects: The cause of the SAT score decline (democratization vs. system quality).

cases.name: The Netherlands (anomaly)
cases.description: The Netherlands achieved high average science rankings (3rd of 17) but had a poor 'fairness score' (16% of schools below par), the worst in northern continental Europe. The author attributes this to the lack of a common core curriculum and public funding of private schools that 'do their own thing.'
key_passages: One fairness score was anomalous—that of the Netherlands. Whereas Holland placed third of seventeen in average science achievement, its fairness score (16 percent of schools below par) was a distant last among the countries of northern continental Europe.
That 16 percent of Dutch schools fell below par was probably owing to the fact that in the Netherlands there are many schools doing their own thing
contestable_aspects: Causal link between Dutch school autonomy and the lack of 'fairness' despite high achievement.

cases.name: The United States (noncore decline)
cases.description: A prime example of a noncore country experiencing significant relative decline in science achievement rankings (7th to 15th) and high school-level inequality (30% of schools below par).
key_passages: In the 1970 study, the United States ranked seventh out of seventeen countries; in the 1980s it ranked fifteenth out of seventeen
Among the noncore countries, the percentages of schools below par were: ... and the United States, 30 percent.
contestable_aspects: Whether the decline is caused by the lack of a core curriculum or other social/economic factors.

cases.name: The Netherlands (IEA 1988)
cases.description: The Netherlands showed a high average science achievement but a very low fairness score (16% schools below par). Hirsch attributes this to the lack of a common core curriculum and a system where many private schools 'do their own thing.'
key_passages: One fairness score was anomalous—that of the Netherlands. Whereas Holland placed third of seventeen in average science achievement, its fairness score (16 percent of schools below par) was a distant last among the countries of northern continental Europe.
That 16 percent of Dutch schools fell below par was probably owing to the fact that in the Netherlands there are many schools doing their own thing

cases.name: Switzerland
cases.description: Switzerland achieves high excellence and fairness without a national curriculum because its population is stable (low mobility). Each canton has its own core curriculum, ensuring children receive a coherent education locally.
key_passages: Switzerland... uses a different core curriculum within each small canton. But since the Swiss do not move very often, the result is a coherent curriculum for each child.

cases.name: France
cases.description: France is cited as a success story for social justice. Its national core curriculum system allows children of immigrants to narrow the achievement gap by the end of 7th grade, relying on school-provided knowledge rather than home background.
key_passages: By the end of seventh grade, the child of a North African immigrant who has attended two years of French preschool (école maternelle) will on average have narrowed the socially induced learning gap.

cases.name: Head Start (USA)
cases.description: A US preschool program that provides initial gains but suffers from benefit fade-out because it lacks academic rigor and is followed by an incoherent public school system.
key_passages: Head Start lasts three hours, is staffed by nonprofessionals, and is nonacademic.
Children fortunate enough to attend Head Start must enter a public school system that is incoherent and fragmented.
contestable_aspects: The characterization of Head Start as 'nonacademic' and staffed by 'nonprofessionals'.

thinkers.name: Thomas Jefferson
thinkers.key_idea: Universal education in history and useful facts is necessary to qualify the people as guardians of their own liberty and prevent government degeneracy.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch uses Jefferson as the foundational authority for the link between shared content-rich curriculum and democratic survival.
key_passages: their minds must he improved to a certain degree.
History by apprizing them of the past will enable them to judge of the future.

thinkers.name: John Dewey
thinkers.key_idea: Education spans the gap between the individual and the social group; community is built on common aims, beliefs, and knowledge.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch notes Dewey's inconsistency but cites his work 'Democracy and Education' to support the necessity of common understanding for community.
key_passages: John Dewey (who said many, sometimes inconsistent things about education) reaffirmed the connections between such common learnings and the goals of community and democracy

thinkers.name: William C. Bagley
thinkers.key_idea: A primary function of formal education in a democracy is to ensure the highest possible level of common culture.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch dedicates the book in part to Bagley and uses his 'common culture' principle as a pillar for Intellectual Capital.
key_passages: A most important function of formal education, especially in a democracy, is to insure as high a level of common culture as possible.

thinkers.name: George Kennan
thinkers.key_idea: Literacy is an economic imperative that makes a populace less malleable and democratic revolutions more durable.
thinkers.engagement: Uses Kennan's geopolitical observation to transition into the domestic need for intellectual capital.
key_passages: Kennan predicted that the democratic revolution would prove more lasting than its predecessor: the Soviet state had been forced by economic imperatives to make its people literate

thinkers.name: Pierre Bourdieu
thinkers.key_idea: Cultural Capital: Social assets (knowledge, style, education) provide the basis for wealth and status.
thinkers.engagement: Cites Bourdieu to validate the sociological claim that knowledge distribution determines social class.
key_passages: The French scholar Pierre Bourdieu has shown that those who possess a larger share of “cultural capital” tend to acquire much more wealth and status

thinkers.name: Gudmund Hernes
thinkers.key_idea: The need for a widely useful and negotiable 'coin of the realm' in national curriculum.
thinkers.engagement: Uses Hernes' Norwegian Education Act as a democratic precedent for requiring shared specific content.
key_passages: As democratic theorists from Jefferson to Gudmund Hernes... have argued, the intellectual currency has to be the widely useful and negotiable coin of the realm.

thinkers.name: James Coleman
thinkers.key_idea: The finding that home background, not school quality, is the primary predictor of student achievement.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch accepts Coleman's data as accurate for the 1960s but argues this finding is an indictment of the 'process-oriented' curriculum of that era, not a permanent law of education.
key_passages: Coleman’s finding is highly consistent with the idea of the importance of intellectual capital, since under the current process-dominated theory it is the home, by default, and not the school that supplies most of the intellectual capital

thinkers.name: Stevenson and Stigler
thinkers.key_idea: The primary problem in teaching is not social/cultural diversity but the variability in children's educational preparation.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch cites their 'shrewd observation' to argue that American classroom incoherence is a structural result of varied preparation rather than student diversity.
key_passages: In fact, a far greater problem is variability in children’s educational background, and thus in their levels of preparation for learning the academic curriculum

thinkers.name: Steven Pinker
thinkers.key_idea: The existence of a biological 'language instinct' in children.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch extends Pinker's concept of a language instinct to a broader 'learning instinct' that drives children to become members of the adult community.
key_passages: Steven Pinker has shown that children are born with a “language instinct.” It is arguable... that the language instinct is part of a more general instinct to learn...

thinkers.name: Sandra Scarr
thinkers.key_idea: The image of background knowledge as 'active tentacles' rather than just hooks.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch credits Scarr for the metaphor and uses her example of a child recognizing a book about the Nile to illustrate how knowledge breeds curiosity.
key_passages: I owe this image to the distinguished child psychologist Sandra Scarr, who provided the following example. A child walks into the school library and sees a book called Exploring the Nile...

thinkers.name: Diane Ravitch
thinkers.key_idea: The history of the 'troubled crusade' of American education, including the curriculum-revision movements of the 1930s.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch uses Ravitch's historical account to support his claim that the shift away from content-oriented curricula began in the 1930s as part of the progressive movement.
key_passages: Beginning in the 1930s as part of the advance of progressive education in the public schools and the colleges of education, there were curriculum-revision movements across the land.24

thinkers.name: Dr. David Wood
thinkers.key_idea: Mobility is an independent and potent predictor of poor school performance, equivalent to poverty or low parental education.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch cites Wood's large-scale study to prove that mobility has an adverse effect on children independent of socio-economic factors.
key_passages: In an interview, Dr. Wood summarized his findings by saying that mobility alone 'is as potent a predictor [of poor school performance] as having parents who are not well educated, or parents who are poor.'

thinkers.name: Herbert Walberg
thinkers.key_idea: Common learning goals and national curricula alleviate the educational disadvantages caused by student mobility.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch uses Walberg's summary of research to support the claim that the negative effects of mobility in the US are a choice of system design, not an inevitability.
key_passages: In a summary of research, Herbert Walberg, citing the work of Bruce C. Straits, states that 'common learning goals, curriculum, and assessment within states... also alleviate the grave learning disabilities faced by children... who move from one district to another.'

thinkers.name: Harold Stevenson
thinkers.key_idea: Math achievement comparisons between nations must control for socioeconomic sampling and ensure tests reflect mandated knowledge.
thinkers.source_work: The Learning Gap; Mathematics Achievement of Chinese, Japanese, and American Children
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch uses Stevenson's methodology to validate the comparison between the US and Japan, asserting that the differences found are due to system differences, not demographic flaws.
key_passages: In conducting this cross-national comparison, Harold Stevenson and his colleagues made sure that the test accurately reflected the knowledge and skills... and that the socioeconomic sampling of students was exactly comparable.

thinkers.name: Walter Loban
thinkers.key_idea: The academic ability gap between advantaged and disadvantaged students widens significantly between grades one and four.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch uses Loban's longitudinal research as foundational evidence for the widening gap in the U.S. system.
key_passages: The longitudinal researches of Walter Loban in the 1960s tracked the acquired learning abilities of disadvantaged and advantaged students as they moved from grade one to grade four and beyond

thinkers.name: Edward Zigler
thinkers.key_idea: Head Start was intended to provide holistic support (health, nutrition, parent involvement) rather than just academic instruction.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch cites Zigler to explain why Head Start was not primarily an academic program and why its academic benefits were secondary and ephemeral.
key_passages: Zigler and his cofounders conceived of Head Start as much more than an educational program. The health, nutrition, motivation, and self-confidence of poor children were deemed to be at least as important to their future well-being as their academic learning.

actors.context: Cited as an organization producing arbitrary and inconsistent conceptual curricular schemes.

actors.context: Cited as an organization producing arbitrary and inconsistent conceptual curricular schemes.

objections_raised.objection: Children need 'all-purpose tools' (critical thinking, love of learning) rather than specific content to adapt to a changing world.
objections_raised.response: This metaphor is contradicted by research; intellectual competencies are not independent of the content they are formed from, and the model has failed for sixty years.

objections_raised.objection: The primary aim of education should be to teach 'critical thinking' techniques and a 'love of learning'—general tools that work regardless of the subject matter.
objections_raised.response: This theory is contradicted by research, has proven ineffective over sixty years, and results in social determinism where only children from wealthy homes gain necessary knowledge.

objections_raised.objection: Classroom incoherence is primarily caused by the social and cultural diversity of American students.
objections_raised.source: Common educational discourse (as identified by Stevenson and Stigler)
objections_raised.response: The greater problem is actually the variability in students' academic preparation and prior educational background.

objections_raised.objection: Placing students in different 'ability' tracks solves the problem of varied student preparation.
objections_raised.response: Tracking does not solve the structural problem because the issue is missing knowledge, not innate ability; even 'smart' people cannot understand something if they lack the requisite background context.

objections_raised.objection: Diversity in children’s social and cultural backgrounds is the greatest problem for teaching in American schools.
objections_raised.response: The far greater problem is the variability in children's educational background and levels of preparation for the academic curriculum.

objections_raised.objection: The principle of local control ensures that localities determine and provide a coherent plan for what children learn.
objections_raised.response: The idea that a coherent content plan exists at the local or school level is a 'gravely misleading myth' and the reality is 'curricular chaos.'

objections_raised.objection: American schools do not need a national curriculum because local districts and schools already determine what children should learn through local control.
objections_raised.response: The idea that local districts have a coherent content plan is a myth; guidelines are typically too vague to ensure any specific content is actually taught across classes.

objections_raised.objection: Adopting a specific, content-rich curriculum (like Core Knowledge) requires abandoning the existing local curriculum.
objections_raised.response: Existing local guidelines are often so empty of specific content that a specific curriculum can be added without 'giving up' anything from the official guidelines.

objections_raised.objection: The 'strand' approach and 'spiraling' provide conceptual depth and unity to the curriculum.
objections_raised.response: In practice, this approach leads to boring repetition of simple topics (like seeds) and massive gaps in essential knowledge (like photosynthesis), while the conceptual schemes themselves remain arbitrary.

objections_raised.objection: Individual teachers can be relied upon to fill in the gaps left by vague district guidelines.
objections_raised.response: Experience shows this is an 'unwarranted assumption' and that gaps in guidelines inevitably become gaps in student knowledge.

objections_raised.objection: Selecting specific U.S. geographical features to teach is a controversial act because it requires excluding other cities or regions.
objections_raised.response: By avoiding the 'controversy' of selection, districts leave teachers with no guidance on what is essential, resulting in a reliance on atlas skills rather than actual knowledge.

objections_raised.objection: Specifying particular facts and content guidelines will generate disagreement and controversy.
objections_raised.response: While true, the alternative (vagueness) results in a system that is inherently unfair to disadvantaged and mobile students, consigning them to slow tracks because the 'rules' of the academic game are never defined.

objections_raised.objection: The academic failure of mobile students is primarily caused by poverty, race, or family structure rather than mobility itself.
objections_raised.source: Implicit in academic discourse on the 'Coleman Report'
objections_raised.response: Dr. David Wood's study of 9,915 children factored out poverty, race, and family status, finding that mobility remained a potent, independent predictor of failure.

objections_raised.objection: The adverse consequences of student mobility are an inevitable byproduct of a mobile society.
objections_raised.response: Evidence from countries with national core curricula shows that these effects are significantly reduced when the curriculum is coherent across districts.

objections_raised.objection: A national curriculum violates state and local responsibilities and autonomy.
objections_raised.response: The principle of local autonomy is not absolute and must be weighed against the 'paramount principles' of educational excellence and social fairness.

objections_raised.objection: International comparisons are 'apples-vs.-oranges' because nations are fundamentally different.
objections_raised.source: Defenders of the U.S. status quo / American Exceptionalists
objections_raised.response: Longitudinal studies tracking the *direction* of change in the same nation over time are methodologically valid, and domestic data shows an absolute decline regardless of international comparisons.

objections_raised.objection: The decline in SAT scores is due to the 'democratization' of the test-taking population (more lower-performing students taking the test).
objections_raised.response: This does not explain the 64% drop in the absolute number of high-scorers (above 600), whose population size remained stable.

objections_raised.objection: Equality of educational opportunity should mean that all students achieve high and equal test scores.
objections_raised.response: Equality of opportunity is not the same as equality of outcome; individual differences in aptitude and effort will lead to different scores, but school-level averages should be consistent.

objections_raised.objection: The academic fade-out in Head Start graduates occurs because the later elementary school environment is not as 'innovative' or supportive as Head Start.
objections_raised.source: U.S. Department of Health and Human Services researchers (1985)
objections_raised.response: This is only half the story; the fade-out is also due to Head Start itself lacking academic coherence and accountability, combined with a similarly incoherent public school system.

objections_raised.objection: We have educated children well in the past without using universal content standards.
objections_raised.response: This is an evasion; the current state of mobility and diversity makes standards more necessary now than in the past.

objections_raised.objection: Knowledge is changing so rapidly that the best approach is to teach children how to learn rather than specific content.
objections_raised.response: Hirsch characterizes this as an evasion that ignores the necessity of intellectual capital for the learning process itself.

objections_raised.objection: It is illegitimate to compare the US with other countries because they are less diverse than we are.
objections_raised.response: Diversity and student mobility actually increase the need for a standardized core curriculum.

objections_raised.objection: A standardized curriculum would obliterate local characteristics and create 'cookie cutter' schools.
objections_raised.response: Extreme localism is outmoded and harmful to children who move frequently.
